title: Linear Search: The Basic Pattern
description: Learn how to search an array for a target value using linear search, written as a reusable function.
congratsText: Great work! You now know how to implement and use linear search as a function.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Implement linear search as a reusable function
  • Explain what happens when a value is not found
  • Trace linear search by hand and count comparisons
  • Recognize when linear search is a reasonable choice

sections.title: Find Your Classmate

quiz.question: If names in a list are in no particular order, what is the only reliable way to find a specific name?
options: Check every name, one at a time, until you find it or reach the end
Jump straight to the middle of the list and guess from there
Sort the list first — searching is never possible on unsorted data
Ask someone else to memorize the entire list in advance

sections.lesson:
Find Your Classmate
If a class list has no particular order, the only reliable way to find a name is to check every one, in order, until you find it. This everyday habit is exactly what linear search does with an array.

sections.title: Linear Search Logic

quiz.question: What does linear search do as it loops through an array?
options: Compares each element to the target, stopping when a match is found
Sorts the array first, then checks only the first element
Compares only the first and last elements to the target
Skips every other element to search faster

sections.lesson:
Linear Search Logic
Linear search loops through the array from start to end, comparing each element to the target. As soon as a match is found, the search stops and reports where it happened.
for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) { // found at index i } }

sections.title: Writing search() as a Function

quiz.question: What does this function return if target is found in the array?
options: The index where the target was found
The value of the target itself
The number 1, meaning true
The total size of the array

sections.lesson:
Writing search() as a Function
Following Week 5's array-function shape, search() takes the array, its size, and the target — and returns the matching index.
int search(int arr[], int size, int target) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) { return i; } } return -1; }

sections.title: Why Return -1 for 'Not Found'

quiz.question: Why does search() return -1 instead of 0 when the target isn't found?
options: 0 is a valid index (the first element), so it can't mean 'not found'
C does not allow functions to return negative numbers otherwise
-1 makes the loop run one extra time to double-check
It has no real reason — any unused number would work the same

sections.lesson:
Why Return -1 for 'Not Found'
Since 0 is a real, valid index — the first element — using it to mean 'not found' would be ambiguous. -1 is never a valid index, so it safely and unambiguously signals failure.

sections.title: Tracing a Search by Hand

quiz.question: How many comparisons does linear search make to find 30 in {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}?
options: 3 — it checks 10, then 20, then finds 30
5 — it always checks every element regardless of position
1 — the target is found immediately
30 — the number of comparisons equals the target's value

sections.lesson:
Tracing a Search by Hand
Tracing by hand means walking through the loop step by step, writing down which index is being compared at each pass. This builds the intuition that a target near the start is found quickly, while one near the end (or missing entirely) takes longer.
arr = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}, target = 30 i=0: 10 == 30? no i=1: 20 == 30? no i=2: 30 == 30? yes — found at index 2 (3 comparisons)

sections.title: The Worst Case: Target Not Present

quiz.question: How many comparisons does linear search make when the target does NOT exist in a 5-element array?
options: 5 — every element must be checked before concluding it's missing
0 — the function knows immediately that it's missing
4 — the last element is never checked
It depends only on the target's numeric value

sections.lesson:
The Worst Case: Target Not Present
When the target isn't in the array at all, linear search still has to check every single element before it can safely conclude that and return -1. This is the worst case for linear search — the maximum possible number of comparisons.

sections.title: Complete Example: Search & Report

quiz.question: What does this program print if target 99 is not in the array?
options: Not found
Found at index 99
Found at index -1
The program crashes

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: Search & Report
A search function is most useful when its result is checked and reported clearly.
int index = search(arr, 5, 99); if (index != -1) { printf("Found at index %d", index); } else { printf("Not found"); }

sections.title: Best Practice: Test Start, End, and Absent Targets

quiz.question: Why should a search function be tested with a target at the very start, the very end, AND a target that isn't present?
options: These are the cases most likely to expose an off-by-one or early-exit bug
C only allows testing three cases per function
The first and last elements are always processed differently by the compiler
It's not actually necessary if the middle elements were tested

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: Test Start, End, and Absent Targets
Bugs in search functions often hide at the edges — an off-by-one loop bound might miss the very last element, or a missing return might cause the wrong index to be reported. Testing the first element, the last element, and a value that isn't there at all catches most of these mistakes.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What is the core behavior of linear search?
options: It checks each element in order until a match is found or the array ends
It only works correctly if the array is already sorted
It jumps directly to the middle of the array first
It compares the size of the array to the target value

sections.lesson:
Recap
Linear search checks each element in order, stopping the moment it finds a match, and reports -1 if it reaches the end without one. Written as a function, it becomes a reusable tool — and the base pattern for every search variation coming next.
int search(int arr[], int size, int target) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] == target) return i; } return -1; }
